skill.name: figma-pencil-fsd-tailwind4
skill.description: Convert Figma or Pencil designs into frontend code plans and implementation guidance using Feature-Sliced Design and Tailwind CSS v4.
skill.summary: Translate design frames into accessible FSD component plans with Tailwind CSS v4 token guidance.
skill.author: JohnWayneeee

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many command execution and weak-crypto patterns, but review found documentation-only Markdown examples rather than executable code. No prompt injection, credential access, or data exfiltration behavior was found. A single external promotional HTTPS link is present, so the skill carries low marketplace risk rather than a publish block.

risk_factor_evidence.assessment: False positive. The evidence is Markdown inline code, HTML examples, headings, or CSS snippets, not executable shell or Ruby code.
True positive as a hardcoded external URL, but it is a user-facing Markdown link and does not trigger an automatic request.

low_findings.title: Markdown Inline Code Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static external-command findings point to Markdown inline code, HTML tag examples, and CSS snippets. The skill contains guidance text, not scripts that execute commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown documentation and CSS examples. I found no runnable script file or command invocation tied to user input.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Signals Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography findings appear on prose lines about Markdown files, design tokens, and Tailwind styling. No cryptographic API or hash algorithm use is present in the cited content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain natural language or styling guidance. There is no MD5, SHA1, crypto library, or security-sensitive hashing behavior.

low_findings.title: Browser Storage Reference Is Architecture Guidance
low_findings.description: The browser storage hit is a reference explaining when a component must be client-side. It does not read localStorage, cookies, or other browser data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line lists browser-only APIs as examples for React component placement. No code accesses browser storage or transmits its contents.

low_findings.title: External Promotional Link Requires Marketplace Review
low_findings.description: The skill includes one hardcoded HTTPS link to a third-party service in a quoted note. This is not automatic network behavior, but it is an external promotion that marketplace reviewers may want to allow or remove.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is directly present in the reviewed file. The risk is limited because it is a Markdown link and no code performs a request.

low_findings.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed After Context Review
low_findings.description: The critical heuristic combined Markdown command-looking text, token terminology, and an external link. Review found no executable code, credential collection, obfuscation, or exfiltration flow.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious signals are semantically unrelated documentation references. I did not find evidence of malicious intent or data movement.

content.user_title: Convert Designs to FSD Tailwind Code
content.value_statement: Design handoff often creates inconsistent frontend structure and styling. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to map Figma or Pencil designs into FSD layers, Tailwind v4 tokens, and accessible components.
actual_capabilities: Maps design frames to Feature-Sliced Design layers and import boundaries.
Translates Figma MCP context into component, token, layout, and asset decisions.
Provides a secondary workflow for Pencil .pen files stored with the project.
Guides Tailwind CSS v4 token usage with CSS-first @theme rules.
Defines Server and Client component placement for frameworks that use that split.
Applies accessibility checks for semantic HTML, focus states, contrast, and keyboard behavior.
limitations: It does not include executable generators or scripts.
Figma guidance depends on an available MCP setup and selected design node.
Pencil support is documented as less mature than the Figma path.
It cannot verify final code unless the host project provides runnable checks.

use_cases.title: Implement a Figma Screen
use_cases.description: Turn one selected Figma frame into a scoped implementation plan with FSD layers, tokens, states, and accessibility checks.

use_cases.title: Standardize Design Handoff
use_cases.description: Keep generated UI aligned with existing primitives, semantic tokens, and local repository architecture rules.

use_cases.title: Refactor Prototype Output
use_cases.description: Split Pencil or prototype-generated UI into reusable shared, entity, feature, widget, page, and app layers.

prompt_templates.title: Map One Frame
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to map the selected design frame into FSD layers. List tokens, reusable primitives, required states, and accessibility checks before writing code.

prompt_templates.title: Implement a Scoped Component
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to implement the selected component. Reuse existing primitives, map design variables to Tailwind v4 tokens, and place files in the correct FSD layer.

prompt_templates.title: Review Server and Client Boundaries
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review the generated UI for Server and Client component placement. Move interactivity to the smallest client leaf and keep data fetching server-side when possible.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Full Design Handoff
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to audit a completed design handoff. Check FSD imports, token usage, primitive reuse, responsive assumptions, accessibility states, and project validation commands.

output_examples.input: A Figma dashboard frame with cards, filters, and an empty state.
output: A layer-by-layer implementation plan for shared primitives, entities, features, widgets, pages, and app routing.
A token mapping note that identifies existing semantic tokens and flags missing design-system values.
A checklist covering loading, empty, error, hover, focus-visible, disabled, and keyboard states.

output_examples.input: A Pencil .pen file for a new settings screen.
output: A scoped plan that treats the .pen file as source material, not a screenshot.
A refactor path from prototype output into reusable FSD modules.
A note about assumptions when responsive breakpoints are not specified.

output_examples.input: A pull request with generated design-to-code components.
output: A review summary focused on token drift, primitive duplication, import direction, and Server or Client boundary mistakes.
Actionable fixes for accessibility gaps such as missing labels, focus rings, and keyboard support.

best_practices: Start with one selected frame, screen, or component and refuse broad whole-file translation.
Read local architecture and design-system rules before applying the skill defaults.
Validate the final result with type checks, linting, tests, accessibility review, and manual keyboard checks when available.
anti_patterns: Creating one monolithic component from a full design screen.
Copying raw colors, spacing, or typography instead of mapping design variables to semantic tokens.
Marking an entire page or app shell as client-side because one small child is interactive.

faq.question: Does this skill generate code automatically?
faq.answer: It provides structured guidance for implementation. It does not include scripts or executable generators.

faq.question: Can it work without Figma MCP?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a Pencil workflow and general design-to-code rules, but Figma MCP gives richer design context.

faq.question: Is Tailwind CSS v4 required?
faq.answer: The token rules target Tailwind CSS v4. For v3 projects, use the repository configuration and skip v4-only guidance.

faq.question: Does it support React Server Components?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for Server and Client boundaries when the host framework supports that model.

faq.question: How does it handle existing design systems?
faq.answer: It tells the assistant to reuse existing primitives and local token names before adding anything new.

faq.question: What should reviewers check after using it?
faq.answer: Review import direction, token mapping, component reuse, accessibility states, responsive assumptions, and project validation results.
